channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.
channel.copyright: Copyright:(C) Tue, 21 Jul 2026 02:13:15 IST Bennett Coleman & Co. Ltd, http://in.indiatimes.com/policyterms/1554651.cms

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Looksmaxxing creator Hullo reportedly returns to TikTok after 15 CSAM charges
item.description: Mason Hull, known online as Hullo, has reportedly resumed posting on TikTok. This comeback follows his recent charges for child sexual abuse material possession. Many social media users are discussing his return and platform policies. His legal case remains ongoing, with no verdict reached yet. The creator's online presence continues to spark debate among the public.
item.dc:creator: Sumit Roy

item.title: Asmongold’s girlfriend Kaise finds cockroach head in streamer’s fridge during live stream
item.description: Popular streamer Asmongold's girlfriend Kaise discovered a cockroach head in his refrigerator. The unexpected find occurred during a recent livestream while preparing food together. Asmongold casually explained the possible accidental presence of the insect part. This incident added to ongoing discussions about the streamer's home condition. Kaise's visit continues to provide memorable moments for viewers.
item.dc:creator: Sumit Roy

item.title: Quote of the day by Aeschylus: 'When one is wise, it's wisest to seem foolish' and a lesson on strategic survival
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Quote of the Day by Seneca: 'We suffer more often in imagination than in reality'
item.description: Seneca, the ancient Stoic philosopher, observed that our imagination often causes us more suffering than actual events. His timeless insight resonates with issues of anxiety faced today. Contemporary research shows our brain struggles to differentiate between real threats and imagined ones. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy leverages this understanding to help individuals challenge distorted thoughts, enabling them to tackle fears more effectively and manage anxiety around potential issues.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Quote of the day by Epicurus: 'Death does not concern us, because as long as we exist...'
item.description: Epicurus, the ancient Greek philosopher, offered a compelling perspective on the fear of death, asserting that it should not concern us since our existence ceases when death arrives. This notion, termed the symmetry argument, challenges the fears associated with dying. Residing in a community known as The Garden, Epicurus sought to promote tranquility through his teachings, which continue to resonate today amidst contemporary worries about life and health.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Quote of the day by Usha Vance: 'There's nothing more exciting than getting to see the world through the eye of kids as they're growing up' and the joys of motherhood
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Scientists tracked endangered Australian cockatoos for three breeding seasons and found...
item.description: The peril faced by Carnaby's black cockatoos is escalating, as pesticide contamination in their food reaches alarming rates. Testing of seed samples revealed that toxic chemicals present are hundreds of times higher than what is legally permissible. This exposure might shed light on the troubling paralysis syndrome afflicting these endangered birds. Furthermore, researchers identified neurotoxic substances banned in various regions of Europe.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Tina Sodhi paid $2,000 for wellness treatment in Florida, she never made it home; doctor charged with manslaughter a year later
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Unlicensed doctor arrested in New York after woman dies from anti-ageing injection
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 'Will not be arrested in US': Trump responds to Mamdani's Netanyahu remarks
item.description: Donald Trump stated Benjamin Netanyahu will not face arrest in the United States. New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani considered legal actions against the Israeli leader. Netanyahu faces an International Criminal Court arrest warrant for alleged crimes. Mamdani believes the Prime Minister belongs in The Hague for his actions. Trump defended Netanyahu's fight against Iran and criticized its leadership.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Scientists found an anemone that can distinguish helpful bacteria from harmful bacteria without...
item.description: Sea anemones distinguish helpful bacteria from harmful invaders without antibodies. Specialized cell structures engulf and destroy foreign bacteria while preserving beneficial microbes. Gene editing revealed a key gene regulating this selective bacterial identification process. This discovery challenges long-held assumptions about early animal immune system evolution. These primitive pathways offer insights into ancient immune memory and maternal transfer.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 16 days after Nolan Wells' death, no one knows what exactly happened to the Mississippi footballer who went to Horn Island with friends
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 'Cherry-picking numbers': Trump admin slams report claiming Pentagon withheld data on troop injuries in Iran war
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Canadian wildfire smoke linked to fewer sightings of 40 bird species in New York
item.description: A new University at Buffalo study has found that smoke from Canadian wildfires may have made many bird species harder to spot in New York State during the 2021–2023 breeding seasons. Researchers linked higher levels of wildfire-related PM2.5 air pollution with fewer sightings of 40 bird species. They say the birds may have changed their behaviour rather than disappeared, highlighting the need to consider air quality when monitoring wildlife.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: After years of living in a trailer, a couple self-built their 3,600-square-foot home for $410K
item.description: Allison Brown and her husband spent six years living in a 1,500-square-foot trailer in South Dakota to keep their housing costs low and save money. Those savings later helped them build their own 3,600-square-foot home for about $410,000 by managing the project themselves. They also turned their old trailer into an Airbnb, creating an additoinal source of income.
item.dc:creator: TOI Real Estate Desk

item.title: Father saved for years so that I could study in America but...: Woman's 'American Dream' turns into 'Make in India'
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: How wild snapdragons tune colors for bees
item.description: Wild snapdragons showcase an impressive ability to adjust their petal colors with the help of seven genes, aimed at attracting bees. These vibrant flowers utilize this precise color calibration as visual cues for their pollinators. Researchers discovered that these genetic variations create subtle yet significant differences, which are critical for survival in narrow hybrid zones where less effective colors are less likely to thrive.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: A human-bred aquarium fish prized for its bright colour has escaped into a Philippines crater lake
item.description: Flowerhorn cichlids, bred by humans, have found their way into Lake Sampaloc, a crater lake in the Philippines. Their presence poses a significant threat to the local ecosystem, competing aggressively with native ayungin fish and disrupting aquaculture. Experts raise alarms about the potential for disease transmission and the rapid expansion of this invasive species, underscoring a pressing global issue of domesticated animals affecting natural habitats.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Trump scores own-goal as World Cup overflows with politics
item.dc:creator: Chidanand Rajghatta

item.title: A floating 'igloo' research station has left France for Arctic, where it will drift with polar ice
item.description: A floating research station shaped like a giant aluminium igloo has left France on a two-year Arctic mission to study biodiversity and the effects of climate change. The station will drift with sea ice while an international team of scientists collects more than 10,000 samples despite extreme cold, months of darkness and the risk of polar bear encounters.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
